title: How Black Holes Evaporate: Hawking Radiation Explained

slides.heading: How Black Holes Evaporate
slides.body: The quantum secret that makes even the universe's darkest objects slowly disappear

slides.heading: Black Holes Aren't Forever
slides.body: Classical physics said black holes only grow — nothing escapes their gravity. In 1974, Stephen Hawking overturned this idea, showing that black holes slowly lose mass and eventually vanish entirely.

slides.heading: The Quantum Vacuum Isn't Empty
slides.body: Quantum mechanics reveals that 'empty' space is actually filled with pairs of virtual particles constantly popping in and out of existence. This fleeting activity is the key to Hawking's discovery.

slides.heading: Particle Pairs at the Event Horizon
slides.body: Near a black hole's event horizon, a virtual particle pair can be split apart. One particle falls inward while the other escapes as real radiation — energy the black hole must pay for from its own mass.

slides.heading: Temperature from Gravity
slides.body: Hawking showed that black holes radiate like a hot object — they have a temperature. The smaller the black hole, the hotter it is. A stellar-mass black hole is far colder than the cosmic microwave background, making detection nearly impossible today.

slides.heading: The Evaporation Timeline
slides.body: As a black hole loses mass it heats up, radiating faster and faster. A black hole the mass of the Sun would take 10⁶⁷ years to fully evaporate — far longer than the current age of the universe (13.8 billion years).

slides.heading: Primordial Black Holes & Explosions
slides.body: Tiny 'primordial' black holes formed in the Big Bang could have evaporated by now. In their final seconds they release an enormous burst of high-energy gamma rays — astrophysicists are actively hunting for these flashes.

slides.heading: The Information Paradox
slides.body: If a black hole evaporates completely, what happens to the information about everything it swallowed? Quantum mechanics says information can't be destroyed. This unresolved puzzle — the black hole information paradox — drives cutting-edge physics research today.

slides.heading: Where Quantum Meets Gravity
slides.body: Hawking radiation sits at the boundary of two great theories: quantum mechanics and general relativity. Understanding it fully may require a complete theory of quantum gravity — the holy grail of modern physics.

slides.heading: The Universe Always Finds a Way
slides.body: Even the most inescapable objects in the cosmos slowly surrender to quantum physics. Hawking radiation reminds us that reality at its deepest level is stranger — and more beautiful — than we imagined.
